join_order.output: orders_joined(~1.44M rows) with o_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority
lineitem_joined(~4.3M rows) with l_orderkey, l_extendedprice, l_discount, o_orderdate, o_shippriority

logical_plan.aggregation: partitioned_hash_map(64 partitions) on (l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority), SUM(l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount))

filter_pushdown.note: dictionary-encoded int32 comparison; look up 'BUILDING' code from c_mktsegment_dict.txt first
applied via zone map skip + per-row filter during scan; table is sorted by o_orderdate enabling zone map efficiency
applied via zone map skip + per-row filter during scan; table is sorted by l_shipdate

notes: Star schema: filter dimension (customer) first, build bitset, probe bridge (orders), build hash map, probe fact (lineitem)
Dense custkey domain [1,1500000] → bitset is O(1) lookup with perfect cache behavior vs hash probing
Orders zone map covers ~50% blocks skip on o_orderdate; lineitem zone map covers ~45% blocks skip on l_shipdate
Join selectivity: ~1/5 of orders match customer filter (c_mktsegment cardinality=5)
Aggregation groups bounded by distinct qualifying o_orderkey values (~1.44M upper bound)

data_structures.customer_filter: bitset(1500001) — dense integer keys c_custkey in [1,1500000]; set bit at position c_custkey for qualifying rows
data_structures.orders_join_build: compact_hash_map(pre_sized, capacity=2^22=4194304) keyed on o_orderkey (int32), value={o_orderdate:int32, o_shippriority:int32}; open-addressing with Robin Hood hashing
data_structures.orders_join_bloom: bloom_filter(~1.44M entries, 18MB, ~1% FP rate) built alongside orders hash map; checked before every lineitem probe to skip non-matching l_orderkey
data_structures.aggregation: partitioned_hash_map(64 partitions by l_orderkey % 64); each partition is a compact_hash_map keyed on (l_orderkey:int32, o_orderdate:int32, o_shippriority:int32), value=int64_t revenue_sum
data_structures.topk: min_heap(size=10) per thread for parallel top-10; merged into global top-10 at end

phases.note: Scan customer in parallel (1.5M rows / 64 threads = ~23K rows/thread). Each thread scans its morsel and writes to a thread-local bitset OR shared bitset with atomic set (bitset writes are idempotent — no CAS needed, only cache-line false sharing risk; use 64-byte aligned bitset blocks)

Zone-map prune orders blocks (150 zones, skip zones where max < 1995-03-15 threshold). Scan ~7.2M qualifying rows. Build orders hash map sequentially (1.44M rows at ~150M rows/s ≈ 10ms) OR in parallel with partitioned build (64 partitions, each thread owns partitions). Sequential build preferred since 1.44M rows is small.

Parallel scan of lineitem with zone-map pruning on l_shipdate. Each thread: (1) check bloom filter for l_orderkey, (2) probe orders hash map, (3) compute revenue = l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount), (4) insert/update into thread-local partitioned aggregation hash map. Morsel size: 65536 rows. This is the dominant phase.
Merge 64 thread-local partition maps: for partition P, thread P merges all 64 local[t][P] maps into a single result. Fully parallel, no synchronization needed.

Each thread maintains a min-heap of 10 entries (sorted by revenue DESC, o_orderdate ASC). After aggregation merge, 64 partition threads each find their local top-10. Final merge: partial_sort on 64*10=640 candidates.

parallelism.synchronization: partitioned_no_contention — each thread owns its partition set during aggregation; bloom filter and bitset use idempotent concurrent writes

index_usage.purpose: zone_map_skip_blocks — skip orders blocks where zone max(o_orderdate) < days_since_epoch(1995-03-15); 150 zones over 15M rows = 100K rows/zone
zone_map_skip_blocks — skip lineitem blocks where zone max(l_shipdate) <= days_since_epoch(1995-03-15); 600 zones over 60M rows = 100K rows/zone

indexes_not_used.reason: Customer is fully scanned for mktsegment filter; custkey PK hash index not needed since we iterate all rows and apply predicate
We build a bitset on customer side and probe from orders scan; the multi-value hash index on o_custkey would require random access per customer key and is slower than sequential scan + bitset probe
Lineitem is the probe side; scanning lineitem sequentially with zone map pruning and probing the orders hash map is faster than random-access via the multi-value index (HDD: sequential > random)

scan_details.customer: sequential columnar scan of c_custkey + c_mktsegment only; fused filter + bitset write
scan_details.orders: zone_map_pruned columnar scan of o_custkey + o_orderkey + o_orderdate + o_shippriority; fused filter (bitset probe + date check) + hash map insert
scan_details.lineitem: zone_map_pruned parallel columnar scan of l_orderkey + l_extendedprice + l_discount; fused bloom check + hash probe + revenue compute + aggregation update

hdd_considerations: Sequential columnar scans maximize HDD throughput (avoid random I/O)
Zone map pruning reduces physical I/O: skip ~52% orders blocks, ~45% lineitem blocks
Reading only needed columns (4 from orders, 3 from lineitem) minimizes bytes read
Avoid all random-access index lookups (idx_orders_custkey, idx_lineitem_orderkey) — sequential scan + in-memory hash probe is faster on HDD

phase_breakdown.note: ~7.2M rows scanned after zone skip (~72 zones), 4 columns, 1.44M hash inserts
~33M rows scanned after zone skip (~330 zones), 3 columns, 64 threads, bloom filter + hash probe + aggregation
min-heap operations on ~1.44M aggregated groups, LIMIT 10

estimated_cost.target_vs_umbra: Umbra=64ms; target ~120-150ms is realistic given HDD vs NVMe I/O gap and sequential scan approach
estimated_cost.bottleneck: HDD sequential read bandwidth for lineitem (~33M rows × 3 columns × 4B = ~396MB physical read after zone skipping)
